planting.note: Start indoors early and plant out after frost.

directions: Grow this rose-flowered form exactly like blue hyssop, in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun. Shear lightly after bloom to encourage a second flush of soft pink spikes. Clip the fragrant tops for the pollinator garden or a mild aromatic tea.

planting.note: Raise from seed indoors and transplant near the last frost.

directions: Plant this white-blooming form in gritty, well-drained soil where it forms a tidy little sub-shrub. Prune each spring and again after flowering to keep the growth dense. Harvest the pale flower spikes for bees or dried herbal use as they open in midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in spring or divide established mats.

directions: This showier relative forms a neat mat topped with plump spikes of large purple flowers. Grow it in moist, humus-rich soil and shear spent blooms to tidy the planting and prompt rebloom. Divide clumps every few years in spring to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then start indoors before setting out.

directions: Chill the seed for a few weeks before sowing, since it needs cold to break dormancy. Plant in consistently moist, rich soil at a pond edge or in dappled shade. Harvest the leafy stems dotted with tiny blue flowers when the plant is in full bloom for the calming nervine herb.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant to a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Give this drought-tolerant species full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil, as it resents wet feet. Handsome spikes of purple-blue flowers appear over narrow leaves in summer. The thick yellow roots, harvested from three-year-old plants in fall, are the prized part in traditional medicine.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in damp ground.

directions: Grow this wild mint in consistently moist soil where its running roots can spread, or confine it to a bed. It carries clusters of tiny lilac flowers tucked in the leaf axils rather than in terminal spikes. Cut the strongly menthol-scented stems for the traditional source of natural mint oil.

planting.note: Plant root cuttings in spring; this sterile strain will not seed around.

directions: Bury root cuttings a few inches deep in fertile soil, crown side up. This non-seeding clone stays put, making it the usual choice for a permaculture mulch bank. Cut the leafy growth two to four times a year for a nutrient-rich mulch or liquid feed.

planting.note: A short cold-stratification improves the otherwise slow germination.

directions: Chill the seed briefly, then surface-sow and be patient, as it can be slow to start. Grow in average, well-drained soil in full sun. Harvest the slender flowering spikes in summer as the small pale flowers begin to open.

planting.note: Nick or soak the hard seed coat before sowing to speed germination.

directions: Scarify or soak the hard seed overnight, then sow shallowly in well-drained soil. This legume forms a deep taproot and dislikes soggy ground. Dig the roots in autumn of the third or fourth year, once they have grown long and substantial.

planting.note: Start warm indoors and set out only after frost, as it needs heat and a long season.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and grow the young plants on in heat, as it is frost-tender. In most climates it is raised as an annual in a warm, sunny, well-drained spot. Dig the roots in autumn after the small orange berries form, before the first frost.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring; it makes a rosette the first year and blooms the second.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface and leave it uncovered, as light aids sprouting. It thrives in poor, dry soil and self-sows freely. Fragrant yellow flowers open in the evening of the second summer; collect the ripe seed pods later for oil.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out once nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto warm, moist mix and barely cover it, keeping the surface humid until sprouts appear. Pinch the growing tips often to force a bushy, leafy plant and remove flower spikes if you want continued leaf production. Snip clove-scented leaves and tender stems once the plant is knee-high and again before each bloom flush.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix, cover lightly, and keep moist for even germination of this slower purple-leaved type. Give it full sun and steady warmth to deepen the wine-purple leaf color, pinching tips to encourage branching. Harvest the aromatic purple foliage from midsummer on, cutting stems just above a leaf pair to spur regrowth.

planting.note: This vigorous perennial type benefits from an early indoor start before setting out after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth, barely covering it, and grow on to a sturdy transplant before setting out. This taller, clove-and-lemon scented species can become woody, so pinch early and give it room to bush out. Harvest leaves throughout the warm season; in frost-free areas it overwinters as a small shrub.

subcategory: adaptogen, tender perennial grown as annual

planting.note: A quick, early-flowering type that fruits fast even in shorter seasons.

directions: Sow indoors onto warm mix and lightly cover, transplanting once the weather settles. This fast, free-flowering strain sets seed readily and self-sows in mild gardens. Harvest the fragrant leaves early and often, and let a few plants flower to draw pollinators and save seed.

planting.note: Root cuttings in shallow water or saturated soil once temperatures warm.

directions: Push stem cuttings into mud at a pond margin or in a container standing in a saucer of water. This succulent creeper thrives in constant wetness and full sun, trailing and rooting as it goes. Harvest the small paired leaves and tender stems anytime once the mat is established, and it rebounds quickly after cutting.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed and set plants out in cool spring conditions.

directions: Give seed several weeks of cold, moist chilling before sowing on the surface of gritty, fast-draining mix. This cold-loving alpine wants full sun, lean soil, and sharp drainage; it languishes in heat and humidity. Let plants grow undisturbed for four or five years, then lift the fragrant rose-scented roots in fall for harvest.

planting.note: Autumn-sow chilled seed in shaded, humus-rich forest soil.

directions: Sow stratified seed shallowly in cool, shaded, loose woodland soil rich in organic matter. Like its American cousin it germinates slowly and demands deep shade and even moisture through several growing seasons. Harvest the aromatic roots after five or six years in autumn once the tops yellow, saving berries to sow the next generation.

planting.note: Establish rooted cuttings or stratified seedlings in spring in a partly shaded spot.

directions: Set out rooted cuttings or seedlings in moist, well-drained soil at a woodland edge with morning sun and afternoon shade. This prickly, very cold-hardy shrub is slow to start but tough once established, needing little care beyond mulch. Dig portions of the root and root bark from plants three years or older in fall, leaving the crown to regrow.

planting.note: Plant potted vines in spring at the base of a sturdy trellis in partial shade.

directions: Set the vine in cool, rich, evenly moist soil with its roots shaded and its top free to climb toward light. Provide a strong trellis and grow both a male and female plant nearby for reliable fruit set. Pick the ruby clusters when fully red in late summer; the berries carry sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and pungent notes at once.

planting.note: Nick and soak seed, then direct-sow where the deep taproot can grow undisturbed.

directions: Scarify and soak the hard seed overnight, then sow a half inch deep in deep, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. Because it forms a long taproot it dislikes transplanting, so plant it in its permanent spot. Dig the long yellow-cored roots in fall from plants at least three or four years old, then slice and dry them.

planting.note: Start soaked seed early indoors; provide support for the climbing stems.

directions: Soak the seed a day, then sow warm and grow the seedlings on before setting them near a trellis. The wiry, feathery stems climb and the plant forms a cluster of tuberous roots below ground. Lift the finger-like roots after eighteen months to two years, replanting the crown to continue the clump.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then set young plants where they can twine on support.

directions: Chill the seed before surface-sowing, then grow the twining seedlings on and give them a trellis or shrub to climb. This gentle ginseng relative likes rich, moist, well-drained soil and light shade. Dig the sweet, spindle-shaped roots in autumn from plants two or three years old, then wash and dry them.

planting.note: A high-altitude crop needing cool days and cold-tolerant of light frost.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly in cool, loose, well-drained soil, thinning seedlings to give each root room. It naturally grows in cold highlands, so it needs a long, cool season and tolerates frost but not heat. Harvest the small radish-like storage roots after roughly seven to eight months, then cure them in the sun.

planting.note: Fresh seed needs light and a cool chill; germination is slow and uneven.

directions: Sow fresh seed on the surface, press it in for light, and be patient through slow, staggered germination. Grow plants in rich, moist soil with some shade; they regrow leaves quickly, hence the name tomorrow's leaf. Pick outer stalks and leaves regularly, and prevent flowering to keep the plant productive since it dies after it seeds.

planting.note: Surface-sow the fine seed and set plants out after the soil warms.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto moist mix without covering it and keep it lightly shaded until sprouts appear. Grow plants in rich, well-drained soil with light shade; they spread slowly by rhizome to form a low patch. Dig the plump storage roots in fall of the first or second year, replanting a few pieces to renew the stand.

planting.note: Root cuttings or divisions in spring on a trellis in a contained spot.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant divisions in rich, moist soil against a sturdy support, keeping it contained since it spreads. The twining vine grows quickly and forms swollen tuberous roots over several seasons. Dig the reddish-brown roots in fall from mature plants three or more years old, then slice and dry them.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted stalks after the soil is thoroughly warm in late spring.

directions: Start a fresh stalk by rooting a store-bought base in water, or divide an established clump in spring. Grow in rich, moist soil with full sun and feed regularly for lush blades. Harvest by cutting whole stalks at the base once the swollen bulbous ends are pencil-thick or more.

planting.note: Transplant after all frost danger; this heat-lover needs a long warm season.

directions: Nick and soak the hard seed, then start indoors weeks before setting out in warm soil. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and space generously for the large bushy plants. Harvest the fleshy red calyces once they swell around the seed pods for the tart, crimson tea.

planting.note: Direct-sow after frost, as fennel resents root disturbance from transplanting.

directions: Sow seed directly where plants are to grow, since the taproot dislikes moving. Give it full sun and fertile soil, and keep it away from dill to prevent crossing. Let flowers set seed, then harvest the ripe aromatic seeds for a sweet anise-flavored tea.

planting.note: Start indoors or set out plants after frost for the bright chartreuse foliage.

directions: Surface-sow seed in warmth, as light and steady moisture aid germination. This selection keeps golden-green leaves best in full sun with average, well-drained soil. Pick the anise-scented foliage and lavender flower spikes through summer for herbal tea.

planting.note: A slow-to-bolt strain, so spring sowings hold leaf a little longer before setting seed.

directions: Direct sow a half inch deep in cool soil and thin seedlings to give each plant room to branch. This selection is prized for slower bolting, but for seed harvest simply allow it to flower on schedule. Gather the umbels once the seed heads dry to a light brown and rattle when shaken.

planting.note: One of the slowest bolting types, useful where a long cutting window matters.

directions: Scatter seed a half inch deep in well drained soil and keep evenly moist during germination. Though bred to resist bolting, it will still throw flower stalks in warm weather, which is when you steer it toward seed production. Harvest the dry tan fruits before the heads shatter and store them whole.

planting.note: A dependable slow-bolt variety that also finishes a reliable seed crop.

directions: Sow directly about a half inch deep in a sunny bed once frost danger eases. Water steadily until the plants establish, then let them bolt and set their round green fruits. When the seed heads dry to brown, clip and hang them over a cloth to catch falling seed.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool spring soil where the plants are to bloom and pod.

directions: Sow the black seeds shallowly, about a quarter inch deep, directly in the garden since the plants resent transplanting. After the pale flowers fade they form inflated, horned seedpods that ripen from green to papery tan. Snip the dry pods and shake out the peppery black seeds used in flatbreads and pickles.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in a warm, sunny spot for a summer seed crop.

directions: Direct sow a half inch deep in warm soil and keep lightly moist until the seedlings appear. This legume carries clusters of blue clover-like flowers that mature into small pods. The dried green-brown seed and ground seedpods flavor traditional Alpine and Georgian cheeses and breads.

planting.note: A tall heavy-heading strain grown mainly for pickling seed.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and thin generously, since this variety grows tall and benefits from a sheltered spot. It is selected for large seed umbels, making it a classic choice for dill pickles. Harvest the whole flowering heads as the seed turns from green to brown.

planting.note: A leafy, aromatic strain that is slow to bolt but still sets good seed.

directions: Sow directly a quarter inch deep and keep the soil evenly moist for germination. This selection is prized for abundant, richly flavored foliage, yet allowed to flower it produces plenty of seed. Cut the umbels when the seeds ripen to brown and dry them before storing.

planting.note: A compact, container-friendly dwarf dill that still flowers and seeds.

directions: Direct sow shallowly or grow in a deep pot, thinning to a few sturdy plants. This dwarf, slow-bolting form stays tidy and rarely needs staking. When you want seed, let it bloom and gather the small umbels as they dry to tan.

planting.note: Sow early in cool soil so seed pods fill before summer heat.

directions: Direct sow a quarter to half inch deep in cool spring soil and thin the fast-growing seedlings. The tall, branching plants bloom bright yellow, then form slender pods packed with pungent dark seed. Cut whole plants as the lowest pods dry, then thresh before the pods shatter and scatter seed.

planting.note: Slow biennial started early indoors; seed comes in the second season.

directions: Surface sow the fine seed indoors and keep it warm and moist, as germination is slow and light-dependent. Transplant into rich, consistently damp soil after frost, growing plants on through the season. Left to overwinter where hardy, they bolt the second year and yield the tiny, intense seeds used in spice blends.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out young plants once hard frost has passed.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it just moist until sprouts appear, then grow on in bright light. Set plants in sharply drained, lean soil and shear the whole mound back hard in early spring to keep it dense. Button-like yellow flowers rise above the silver foliage in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a hot, well-drained spot.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty mix and provide warmth and light for even sprouting. Give this green-leaved form full sun and dry, poor soil, cutting it back by half after bloom to prevent it from splitting open. Bright yellow pincushion flowers open on wiry stems in summer.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after the last frost for tidy low edging.

directions: Root softwood cuttings in summer since this compact selection is grown from cuttings to stay uniform. Plant in fast-draining soil and clip lightly to hold a neat cushion for knot gardens and low hedges. Small yellow buttons appear in summer and can be sheared off to favor the silvery foliage.

planting.note: Divide or plant out after frost into gritty, lean soil.

directions: Grow from division or cuttings to keep this selection true, and avoid rich or wet soil that makes it flop. Shear the cushion back after it opens up in midsummer heat to force fresh, feathery silver growth. The threadlike foliage forms a soft dome that is the whole point, so any flowers can be trimmed away.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; expect rhizomatous spread.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed under light and thin seedlings before transplanting. Plant in dry, well-drained ground and be aware that creeping roots can travel, so give it room or a buried barrier. The silvery-white aromatic foliage is cut for fresh and dried arrangements through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; wear gloves when handling.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep warm until germination, then grow seedlings in bright light. Plant in lean, well-drained soil and shear back in spring to maintain the mounded blue-green foliage. Handle with care in sun, as the sap can cause a phototoxic skin rash; dull yellow flowers appear in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; propagate vegetatively for best color.

directions: Take cuttings to keep the intense steel-blue foliage of this compact selection consistent. Grow in poor, sharply drained soil and clip in spring to hold a tidy dome. Wear gloves to avoid skin irritation from the sap, and expect small mustard-yellow flowers in summer.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seeds about an inch deep in poor to average soil for the best flowering. This bushy strain is prized for its cream-marbled variegated foliage that stays showy even between blooms. Snip the bright flowers for salads once they are fully open.

planting.note: Start indoors weeks ahead and set out while weather is still cool.

directions: Sow into flats and keep the seeds cool and dark until they germinate. Transplant into rich, moist soil for a display that peaks in spring and fall. Pinch spent blooms often and pick the large flat flowers to decorate plates and cakes.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring; this cultivar is not grown from seed.

directions: Plant divisions in rich, moist soil with room for good airflow around each clump. This selection is valued for large true-red flowers and strong resistance to powdery mildew. Deadhead spent heads to prolong bloom and harvest the edible florets for garnishes.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring; this hybrid is propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant in full sun with sharp drainage, as soggy winter soil is the main cause of loss. This sturdy hybrid produces dense, powdery-blue bottlebrush spikes that swarm with pollinators. Deadhead lightly and harvest leaves and edible flowers for tea through the season.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of the last frost or direct sow once soil warms.

directions: Cover the slender seeds lightly and keep warm until they sprout in a week or so. These lacy-leaved plants form tidy mounds smothered in small single flowers all summer. The bright lemon-yellow blooms have a citrusy tarragon flavor; pick them fresh for salads.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings outdoors after frost danger has passed.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and barely cover it, keeping the soil warm and moist for even sprouting. The deep purple-red leaves color best in full to partial sun, so give them bright light. Pick leaves regularly and pinch off flower spikes to keep the plant productive and the foliage tender.

planting.note: Start indoors early and plant out when nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface and keep it bright and moist until sprouts appear, as buried seed germinates poorly. This broad-leaved type grows large flat leaves prized for wrapping, so feed lightly and water steadily. Harvest the biggest lower leaves first and remove flower stalks to prolong the season.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after all frost danger passes.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm soil and keep it moist until the seedlings appear. Pinch out the growing tips once plants have several sets of leaves to build a full, branching plant with its signature purple stems and anise scent. Harvest sprigs regularly and remove flower buds to keep the leaves coming.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Surface-sow and press the seed in lightly, keeping it warm and bright for germination. This selection shows green tops with striking purple undersides and a warm cinnamon-like scent. Pinch tips to keep it bushy and harvest leaves from the top, removing flower spikes as they appear.

planting.note: Transplant out once the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. Pinch early to build a bushy plant of this citrus-scented basil used in Thai and Lao dishes. Harvest leaves young and pinch off flower spikes to keep the plant leafy and productive.
